questions.stem: A text message tells Mara that her bank account will be permanently frozen within one hour unless she confirms her login through the link provided. Which pair of psychological tactics is the message relying on?
options: Intimidation and urgency — it threatens a harmful outcome and demands quick action
Recognition and revenge — it appeals to her status and a past grievance
Greed and curiosity — it offers a reward for clicking the link quickly
Politics and beliefs — it pressures her over a cause she supports
questions.rationale: Intimidation threatens a target with negative consequences (the account being frozen), and urgency creates a reason to act quickly (within one hour). Both are the psychological tactics named in 1.1.A.2.

questions.stem: According to the CED, social engineering attacks succeed primarily by doing which of the following?
options: Brute-forcing encrypted passwords stored on a remote server
Using psychological tactics to manipulate users into revealing information or clicking a link
Flooding a wireless channel to block all legitimate network traffic
Exploiting an undocumented zero-day flaw in the operating system
questions.rationale: 1.1.A.1 defines social engineering as employing psychological tactics to manipulate users into revealing sensitive information, downloading a malicious file, or clicking a malicious link.

questions.stem: A caller convinces an employee to read back the one-time code that was just texted to the employee's phone. What is the MOST direct impact described in the CED?
options: The employee's device is now physically jammed and cannot connect to Wi-Fi
The employee's password becomes longer and therefore stronger over time
The adversary can use that authentication code to log in to the service as the victim
The adversary gains the capacity to discover undocumented zero-day vulnerabilities
questions.rationale: 1.1.C.2 states that giving an adversary secure information like a one-time password (OTP) or authentication login code could allow the adversary to log in to a service as the victim.

questions.stem: An attacker chats with a target on social media and casually draws out the names of the target's pets and the city where they grew up. Why is this information valuable to the attacker, per the CED?
options: It lets the attacker jam the frequency range used by the target's home router
It guarantees the attacker can read traffic protected by HTTPS encryption
It allows the attacker to train an AI model to write malware automatically
It is often used as answers to website challenge questions that verify identity
questions.rationale: 1.1.C.1 says personal information such as pets' names and similar details are often used on websites as challenge questions to verify a user's identity, enabling impersonation.

questions.stem: A security analyst reviews login records and sees dozens of failed logins to one account within two minutes, several at 3 a.m., from a device never seen before. Which conclusion is BEST supported?
options: These are routine logins reflecting an account owner who travels frequently
These are signs of an online password attack against the account
These indicate a jamming attack on the building's wireless access point
These show the LLM has leaked the user's training data in a prompt
questions.rationale: 1.2.A.2 lists many failed login attempts over a short duration, login attempts at unusual times, and attempts from unknown devices as signs of an online password attack.

questions.stem: In the online password attack described by the CED, adversaries primarily attempt to log in using which inputs?
options: Common passwords, common password patterns, or stolen passwords
Electromagnetic signals broadcast across the wireless frequency band
Cloned voice samples taken from a target's social media videos
Encrypted DNS queries intercepted from an unencrypted Wi-Fi network
questions.rationale: 1.2.A.1 says that in an online password attack, adversaries try logging in using common passwords, common password patterns, or stolen passwords.

questions.stem: An adversary collects a target's birthday, anniversary, and pets' names, then feeds an automated tool a list of guesses built from that data. Which technique does this BEST illustrate?
options: Setting up an evil twin access point near the target's home
Crafting a prompt that extracts secure information from an LLM
Building a personalized dictionary and submitting passwords automatically
Flooding the target's network with a strong electromagnetic signal
questions.rationale: 1.2.B.2 describes adversaries constructing a dictionary of possible passwords from personal information gathered about a target and using an automated tool to submit them.

questions.stem: A user wants the strongest practical protection in case their password is ever guessed or stolen. Which single control does the CED recommend enabling for this purpose?
options: Choosing a password that includes a family member's name and a birth year
Joining only wireless networks whose names look familiar at a glance
Verifying AI chatbot output against other AI chatbots before trusting it
Multifactor authentication, which requires extra proof of identity beyond the password
questions.rationale: 1.2.C.3 says that when available, users should enable multifactor authentication (MFA), which requires extra proof of identity such as a one-time code in addition to the password.

questions.stem: A teacher wants students to create accounts that resist the personalized dictionary attacks discussed in Unit 1. Which guidance aligns with the CED?
options: Make passwords long, random, and unique, and avoid personally meaningful words or dates
Reuse one strong passphrase across every account to reduce mistakes
Add a two-digit year and a special character to a common dictionary word
Pick a password based on a pet's name so it is easy to remember
questions.rationale: 1.2.C.1 says passwords should be long, random, and unique, and 1.2.C.2 says users should avoid names, dates, or other personally meaningful words or numbers.

questions.stem: A breach is traced to someone who bought a ready-made hacking tool online that exploits a publicly known flaw. How does the CED classify this adversary?
options: A high-skilled adversary capable of discovering undocumented zero-day vulnerabilities
A low-skilled adversary relying on tools made by others that exploit known vulnerabilities
A defender using AI to recommend more secure firewall configurations
A jammer broadcasting electromagnetic interference across the wireless band
questions.rationale: 1.3.A.1 says low-skilled adversaries rely on malicious cyber tools created by others that can be purchased online, and those tools exploit known vulnerabilities.

questions.stem: At a coffee shop, a laptop connects to a network whose name is identical to the shop's official Wi-Fi, but it was actually set up by an attacker nearby. Which attack is occurring?
options: A war driving attack that detects wireless beacons from a moving vehicle
A jamming attack that floods the area with an electromagnetic signal
An online password attack using a dictionary of personal information
An evil twin attack using a rogue access point with a matching SSID
questions.rationale: 1.3.B.1 describes an evil twin: an adversary sets up their own wireless access point with an SSID similar or identical to a target network so victims unknowingly connect, allowing the adversary to capture their traffic.

questions.stem: During an event, no one in a building can reach the wireless network because an attacker is broadcasting a strong signal in the same frequency range as the access point. This is BEST described as which combination?
options: A war driving attack, which is a form of credential theft
An evil twin attack, which is a form of social engineering
A jamming attack, which is a form of denial-of-service (DoS) attack
A password attack, which is a form of wireless reconnaissance
questions.rationale: 1.3.B.2 says a jamming attack floods an area with a strong EM signal in the network's frequency range, preventing legitimate traffic; an attack that prevents access to resources is a denial-of-service (DoS) attack.

questions.stem: A traveler must use an untrusted public Wi-Fi network and wants to keep their browsing traffic private from the network and service provider. Which CED-recommended action fits, and what is its key limitation?
options: Use a VPN to encrypt traffic, but the VPN provider itself can still view that traffic
Use a jamming device to block other users, but it disables their own connection
Connect to any network with a familiar name, but it cannot protect DNS queries
Disable HTTPS to speed up browsing, but the connection becomes easier to read
questions.rationale: 1.3.C.3 says a VPN encrypts all traffic to the VPN operator's system, preventing the service provider from viewing it, but notes the VPN provider can view the traffic.

questions.stem: Before joining a public wireless network, a student checks that the network name exactly matches the one the venue posted. Which protective principle does this follow?
options: Jam any nearby networks that share a similar service set identifier
Verify that the network's name exactly matches the network they intend to join
Enter sensitive data only into AI chatbots that promise not to store it
Assume all internet protocols are unencrypted and avoid all browsing
questions.rationale: 1.3.C.1 says individuals should verify that the name of any wireless network they join exactly matches the name of the network they intend to join.

questions.stem: An employee receives a flawless email, in perfect native-sounding language, asking them to update payroll details. The team's old advice—'watch for awkward grammar'—no longer helps. Which CED point explains why?
options: Adversaries now jam the email server's frequency to bypass spam filters
Generative AI lets adversaries craft phishing messages that read like a native speaker wrote them
Encrypted HTTPS traffic prevents defenders from reading any email content
A VPN provider rewrote the email to remove the awkward language automatically
questions.rationale: 1.4.A.2 says adversaries can use generative AI tools like LLMs to craft phishing messages in any language that read as though written by a native speaker, removing unnatural language as a tell.

questions.stem: A finance worker gets a video call that looks and sounds exactly like their CEO, urgently authorizing a large wire transfer. Which AI-augmented attack technique does this represent?
options: An online password attack using stolen credentials from the CEO
A war driving attack detecting the CEO's wireless beacon
A jamming attack that floods the call with electromagnetic noise
A digital avatar built from voice and image samples to impersonate a person
questions.rationale: 1.4.A.1 says adversaries can use AI tools that leverage existing voice and image samples to create a digital avatar that impersonates someone over the phone or on a video call, which can lead to financial loss.

questions.stem: The CED notes that adversaries can use AI-enhanced coding tools chiefly to do which of the following?
options: Write new malware, modify code for malicious activity, or find flaws in large code bases
Encrypt all of a victim's traffic to a trusted VPN operator's system
Establish a shared secret phrase between two close family members
Verify AI output against reputable, stable, non-AI-based sources
questions.rationale: 1.4.A.6 says adversaries can use AI-enhanced coding tools to help write new malware, modify existing application code to perform malicious activities, or find vulnerabilities in large code bases.

questions.stem: A family worries about deepfake phone scams that imitate a relative's voice. Which two CED protections most directly defend against a cloned-voice impersonation attempt?
options: Jam the caller's frequency and disable HTTPS on the family's devices
Establish a shared secret phrase and enable multifactor authentication on accounts
Reuse one memorable password and post fewer photos to social media
Connect only through evil twin networks and avoid all public Wi-Fi
questions.rationale: 1.4.B.1 recommends establishing a shared secret phrase to verify identities in high-stakes situations, and 1.4.B.2 says MFA can prevent access even if an adversary clones a voice to defeat voice authentication.

questions.stem: An employee is tempted to paste a confidential client contract into a public AI chatbot to summarize it. Which CED warning applies MOST directly?
options: Chatbots automatically jam any network the contract is transmitted over
Chatbots convert pasted text into an unbreakable evil twin signal
Some AI tools feed user input back into the model, and adversaries could extract that data
Pasting text into a chatbot strengthens the company's firewall rules
questions.rationale: 1.4.B.3 says personal or sensitive data should not be entered into AI tools because some feed user input back into the model for training, and adversaries could extract data included in prompts.

questions.stem: A security team uses an AI tool that proposes new firewall rules and detection rules for their network. What does the CED say should happen before these are put into production?
options: The rules should be deployed instantly since AI output is always reliable
The rules should be shared publicly so other organizations can copy them
The recommendations should be reviewed by a knowledgeable security or detection professional first
The recommendations should be entered into a chatbot to be rewritten again
questions.rationale: 1.5.A.1 and 1.5.A.3 both stress that AI recommendations for security configurations and detection rules should always be reviewed by a knowledgeable security technician or detection engineer before being implemented.

questions.stem: A network generates millions of digital events daily, far too many for analysts to inspect by hand. According to the CED, how do AI-powered tools help a SOC handle this volume?
options: They analyze events to sort likely-malicious activity from harmless activity and alert personnel
They jam the network so that fewer events are generated each day
They replace all human analysts and remove the need for any review
They encrypt every event with a VPN so adversaries cannot see them
questions.rationale: 1.5.B.2 says AI tools can be trained to quickly analyze events and sort likely-malicious from harmless ones, and 1.5.B.3 says they can alert human personnel or take corrective actions when malicious activity is detected.

questions.stem: Which statement BEST captures the CED's stated benefit of AI-powered threat detection and response for defenders?
options: It guarantees that no malicious event will ever reach the network again
It lets teams catch malicious activity and intervene quickly to prevent loss and damage
It removes the need to review any AI-suggested detection rules before use
It encrypts traffic so that the VPN provider cannot view network events
questions.rationale: 1.5.B.4 says AI-powered tools enable threat-detection and response teams to catch malicious activity and intervene quickly to prevent loss, harm, damage, and destruction to digital infrastructure and data.

partII.instructions: Answer in complete sentences; cite specific tactics, attacks, or controls.

questions.prompt: Devon receives a text message that appears to come from his bank: 'SECURITY ALERT: Suspicious charge detected. Your account will be locked in 30 minutes unless you verify your identity now: secure-bank-verify.com/login.' Devon taps the link, lands on a page that looks like his bank, and enters his username, password, and the one-time code his bank just texted him.
parts: (a) Identify the social-engineering tactics this message uses and explain how each influences Devon's behavior.
(b) Explain the specific impact of Devon entering his one-time code on that page.
(c) Recommend ONE control Devon's bank or Devon could use to reduce the damage of attacks like this, and justify why it helps.
sample: (a) The message uses intimidation and urgency. Intimidation works by threatening a negative consequence—Devon's account being locked—which leverages a natural human aversion to negative outcomes and makes him act out of fear (1.1.A.2, 1.1.B.2). Urgency works through the 30-minute deadline, which pressures Devon to respond quickly and prevents him from stopping to consider whether the request is reasonable or safe (1.1.A.2, 1.1.B.3).

(b) By entering the one-time code on the fake page, Devon hands the adversary secure authentication information. As described in 1.1.C.2, giving an adversary a one-time password or authentication login code can allow the adversary to log in to the real banking service as Devon, so the attacker can now access his actual account.

(c) Devon (and his bank) should rely on the principle behind multifactor authentication and treat the verification path carefully: in general MFA requires extra proof of identity beyond a password (1.2.C.3), but this case shows MFA codes must never be typed into a link from an unsolicited message. A strong recommendation is to ignore links in alert texts and instead log in through the bank's known app or website; combined with MFA, this prevents a phishing page from capturing both the password and the live code at once.
rubric: 2 pts: Names BOTH intimidation (threat of account lock) and urgency (the deadline) as the tactics in part (a).
2 pts: Explains how each tactic influences behavior—fear of negative consequences and pressure to act quickly without evaluating the request (1.1.B.2, 1.1.B.3).
2 pts: Part (b) correctly identifies that surrendering the one-time code lets the adversary log in to the service as the victim (1.1.C.2).
2 pts: Part (c) recommends a valid control (e.g., MFA per 1.2.C.3 and/or not following links in unsolicited messages) and justifies why it reduces harm.

questions.prompt: While working from a busy airport, Priya connects her laptop to a Wi-Fi network named 'Airport_Free_WiFi,' the same name shown on the airport's signs. An attacker sitting nearby has set up a rogue access point broadcasting that exact name. Priya then logs in to several accounts whose passwords follow her usual pattern: a pet's name, the year she adopted the pet, and an exclamation point.
parts: (a) Identify the wireless attack the nearby attacker is conducting and explain how it works.
(b) Explain why Priya's password habit makes her especially vulnerable, referencing how adversaries exploit weak authentication.
(c) Recommend TWO concrete protective actions—one for the network risk and one for the password risk—and justify each.
sample: (a) The attacker is running an evil twin attack. As described in 1.3.B.1, the adversary sets up their own wireless access point with an SSID identical or similar to the legitimate network; victims may unknowingly connect to the evil twin, which lets the adversary capture their network traffic. (The adversary still cannot read traffic protected by an encrypted protocol like HTTPS.)

(b) Priya's passwords follow a common, predictable pattern—a meaningful word plus a significant date plus a special character—which is exactly the weakness described in 1.2.B.1. Worse, the components (a pet's name and an adoption year) are personal facts an adversary can gather and feed into an automated dictionary tool to guess her passwords (1.2.B.2), so her authentication is weak even before any traffic is captured.

(c) For the network risk, Priya should verify that the network name exactly matches the one she intends to join and use a VPN, which encrypts all her traffic to the VPN operator so the local attacker cannot read it, even on the evil twin (1.3.C.1, 1.3.C.3). For the password risk, she should create long, random, unique passwords—ideally with a password manager—and avoid names and dates (1.2.C.1, 1.2.C.2), and enable MFA so a guessed password alone is not enough (1.2.C.3).
rubric: 2 pts: Part (a) names the evil twin attack and explains the rogue AP with a matching SSID capturing victim traffic (1.3.B.1).
2 pts: Part (b) identifies the predictable password pattern as a weak-authentication habit (1.2.B.1).
2 pts: Part (b) connects the personal details to a personalized dictionary / automated guessing attack (1.2.B.2).
2 pts: Part (c) gives a valid network mitigation with justification (verify exact network name and/or use a VPN, 1.3.C.1/1.3.C.3).
1 pt: Part (c) gives a valid password mitigation with justification (long/random/unique, avoid personal data, or MFA—1.2.C.1/1.2.C.2/1.2.C.3).

questions.prompt: A regional hospital's IT director is worried about two trends. First, finance staff have started receiving phone calls in which a caller sounds exactly like a senior executive and urgently requests fund transfers. Second, the security team is overwhelmed by the millions of network events generated each day and cannot review them all by hand. The director is considering how AI plays a role on both the attacker's side and the defender's side.
parts: (a) Explain how an adversary could use AI to produce the convincing executive phone calls, and describe the potential impact.
(b) Recommend a protective measure the hospital staff could adopt against this voice-impersonation threat, and justify it.
(c) Explain how the security team could leverage AI-powered tools to handle the flood of daily events, including the appropriate role of human reviewers.
sample: (a) The adversary can use AI-powered tools that leverage existing voice (and image) samples of the executive to build a digital avatar that impersonates them over the phone or on a video call (1.4.A.1). Because many staff and organizations trust a familiar voice—and some use voice-based authentication—this impersonation can lead to financial loss or the disclosure of sensitive information when staff act on the fraudulent transfer requests (1.4.A.1).

(b) Staff should establish a shared secret word or phrase known only to the two legitimate parties and use it to verify identity in high-stakes situations like fund transfers (1.4.B.1). Enabling multifactor authentication is also valuable, since even if an adversary clones a voice to defeat voice authentication, a second factor can stop them from gaining access (1.4.B.2). These controls work because a cloned voice cannot supply a secret it never knew or a separate authentication factor.

(c) The security team can deploy AI-powered tools trained to quickly analyze the millions of daily digital events and sort the ones likely to be malicious from the harmless ones (1.5.B.1, 1.5.B.2). Those tools can alert human cybersecurity personnel or take specific corrective actions when malicious activity is detected (1.5.B.3), letting the team catch threats and intervene quickly to prevent loss and damage (1.5.B.4). Humans remain essential: AI-suggested detection rules should always be reviewed by a knowledgeable detection engineer before being added to the system (1.5.A.3).
rubric: 2 pts: Part (a) explains AI building a digital avatar from voice/image samples to impersonate the executive (1.4.A.1).
1 pt: Part (a) states a realistic impact such as financial loss or disclosure of sensitive information (1.4.A.1).
2 pts: Part (b) recommends a valid defense (shared secret phrase per 1.4.B.1 and/or MFA per 1.4.B.2) with justification.
2 pts: Part (c) explains AI sorting/triaging events and alerting or taking action (1.5.B.2/1.5.B.3) to enable fast response (1.5.B.4).
2 pts: Part (c) notes the human role—AI recommendations/detection rules must be reviewed by a knowledgeable professional (1.5.A.3).
